His persistent petitions led to the Shinshū sect’s withdrawal from the Campaign. Similarly, in 1876, the Kurozumikyō and Shintō Shūseiha movements also received permission to withdraw. These withdrawals caused resentment among Campaign leaders, who viewed these sects as undermining Shinto’s image of unity.<ref name=":0" />
== Shift to Shinto Dominance == With the departure of the Buddhists, the Campaign became distinctly "Shinto" in character. In response, the government established the Shinto Office (神道事務局, Shintō jimukyoku) in 1875, headed by the Chief Priest of the Ise Shrines, to replace the Great Teaching Institute. This marked the beginning of state management over Shinto shrines, contrasting with the autonomous operation of Buddhist and other religious organizations outside the Campaign.<ref name=":0" />
== Financial Challenges and Formation of Shrine Churches == By 1876, there were over 10,000 National Instructors, but the lack of public funding posed significant challenges. The central government’s decision not to provide financial support led to the development of a new type of Shinto organization: the Shrine Church (教会, kyōkai). These churches were attached to shrines and organized by territory-based parishes, independently funded by their members. Priest-Instructors led these churches, promoting Shinto funerals and ancestral rites to parishioners, thus reducing reliance on Buddhist temples. However, performing funerals was problematic for Shinto priests due to the pollution of death, which conflicted with Shinto purity laws. By serving as Instructors, priests could separate their funeral duties from their shrine roles, alleviating this issue.<ref name=":0" />
== Jingūkyōkai and Its Expansion == The Ise Grand Shrines played a crucial role in this new organizational model by establishing Jingūkyōkai in 1872. Organized by Urata Nagatami and Tanaka Yoritsune, Jingūkyōkai included ministers from former pilgrim masters who also became Instructors. By 1875, Jingūkyōkai had 304,704 members and operated a network of 1,600 preaching stations nationwide, often located in existing shrines. The organization presented Amaterasu as an agricultural deity and distributed talismans and almanacs from the Ise Grand Shrines. Jingūkyōkai ministers frequently addressed large audiences, numbering in the thousands.<ref name=":0" />
== Ideological and Financial Contradictions == Despite these efforts, the absence of public funding revealed contradictions in the rhetoric of shrines as public institutions providing national rites and creeds. The appointment of the Ise Shrines’ Chief Priest to head the Shinto Office implied that Shinto should center on Ise, a notion challenged by the Head Priest of the Izumo Shrine, Senge Takatomi, during the Pantheon Dispute.<ref name=":0">https://books.google.ca/books?id=ybljDQAAQBAJ&pg=PA380&lpg=PA380&dq=While+the+shrine+priests+struggled+to+establish+these+churches+in+order+to+support+their+proselytizing+as+Instructors,+however,+the+absence+of+public+funding+revealed+a+contradiction+with+the+rhetoric+of+shrines+as+kokka+no+s%C5%8Dshi,+public+facilities+providing+the+nation%E2%80%99s+rites+and+creed.+If+the+funding+had+to+be+arranged+entirely+on+a+private+basis,+then+what+foundation+was+there+for+claiming+that+shrines+had+a+public+status?+The+Shinto+Office+(Shint%C5%8D+jimukyoku)+was+supposed+to+smooth+these+bumps+in+the+road,+but+the+appointment+of+the+Ise+Shrines%E2%80%99+Chief+Priest+as+its+head+implied+that+Shinto+as+a+whole+should+center+on+Ise.+The+Head+Priest+of+the+Izumo+Shrine,+Senge+Takatomi+(1845%E2%80%931918),+challenged+that+idea+in+the+Pantheon+Dispute.&source=bl&ots=ePJp6y1JXS&sig=ACfU3U1vvWlDLshv_64QypQr5vdEAXk_yA&hl=en&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwiV-uf164yHAxVGn44IHXk1DcsQ6AF6BAgLEAM#v=onepage&q=While%20the%20shrine%20priests%20struggled%20to%20establish%20these%20churches%20in%20order%20to%20support%20their%20proselytizing%20as%20Instructors%2C%20however%2C%20the%20absence%20of%20public%20funding%20revealed%20a%20contradiction%20with%20the%20rhetoric%20of%20shrines%20as%20kokka%20no%20s%C5%8Dshi%2C%20public%20facilities%20providing%20the%20nation%E2%80%99s%20rites%20and%20creed.%20If%20the%20funding%20had%20to%20be%20arranged%20entirely%20on%20a%20private%20basis%2C%20then%20what%20foundation%20was%20there%20for%20claiming%20that%20shrines%20had%20a%20public%20status%3F%20The%20Shinto%20Office%20(Shint%C5%8D%20jimukyoku)%20was%20supposed%20to%20smooth%20these%20bumps%20in%20the%20road%2C%20but%20the%20appointment%20of%20the%20Ise%20Shrines%E2%80%99%20Chief%20Priest%20as%20its%20head%20implied%20that%20Shinto%20as%20a%20whole%20should%20center%20on%20Ise.%20The%20Head%20Priest%20of%20the%20Izumo%20Shrine%2C%20Senge%20Takatomi%20(1845%E2%80%931918)%2C%20challenged%20that%20idea%20in%20the%20Pantheon%20Dispute.&f=false</ref>

revision.text:
[[ja:神社教会]] '''Shrine Churches (神社教会, jinja kyōkai)''' were a kind of Shinto organization that existed in the [[Meiji period]]. They emerged as a significant organizational model in Japan during the Meiji period, influenced by the interplay between Buddhist and Shinto clergy and the policies of the [[Great Promulgation Campaign]].<ref name=":0" />
== Early Collaboration and Withdrawal == In the early years of the Great Promulgation Campaign, Buddhist and Shinto clergy worked together to promote national religious unity. However, this cooperation ended in 1875 when Buddhist sects began to withdraw. Shimaji Mokurai, an influential Shinshū priest, played a key role in this shift. After traveling to Europe, Mokurai became convinced that the state should remain separate from religion and tolerate diverse religious beliefs. His persistent petitions led to the Shinshū sect’s withdrawal from the Campaign. Similarly, in 1876, the Kurozumikyō and Shintō Shūseiha movements also received permission to withdraw. These withdrawals caused resentment among Campaign leaders, who viewed these sects as undermining Shinto’s image of unity.<ref name=":0" />
== Shift to Shinto Dominance == With the departure of the Buddhists, the Campaign became distinctly "Shinto" in character. In response, the government established the Shinto Office (神道事務局, Shintō jimukyoku) in 1875, headed by the Chief Priest of the Ise Shrines, to replace the Great Teaching Institute. This marked the beginning of state management over Shinto shrines, contrasting with the autonomous operation of Buddhist and other religious organizations outside the Campaign.<ref name=":0" />
== Financial Challenges and Formation of Shrine Churches == By 1876, there were over 10,000 National Instructors, but the lack of public funding posed significant challenges. The central government’s decision not to provide financial support led to the development of a new type of Shinto organization: the Shrine Church (教会, kyōkai). These churches were attached to shrines and organized by territory-based parishes, independently funded by their members. Priest-Instructors led these churches, promoting Shinto funerals and ancestral rites to parishioners, thus reducing reliance on Buddhist temples. However, performing funerals was problematic for Shinto priests due to the pollution of death, which conflicted with Shinto purity laws. By serving as Instructors, priests could separate their funeral duties from their shrine roles, alleviating this issue.<ref name=":0" />
== Jingūkyōkai and Its Expansion == The Ise Grand Shrines played a crucial role in this new organizational model by establishing Jingūkyōkai in 1872. Organized by Urata Nagatami and Tanaka Yoritsune, Jingūkyōkai included ministers from former pilgrim masters who also became Instructors. By 1875, Jingūkyōkai had 304,704 members and operated a network of 1,600 preaching stations nationwide, often located in existing shrines. The organization presented Amaterasu as an agricultural deity and distributed talismans and almanacs from the Ise Grand Shrines. Jingūkyōkai ministers frequently addressed large audiences, numbering in the thousands.<ref name=":0" />
== Ideological and Financial Contradictions == Despite these efforts, the absence of public funding revealed contradictions in the rhetoric of shrines as public institutions providing national rites and creeds. The appointment of the Ise Shrines’ Chief Priest to head the Shinto Office implied that Shinto should center on Ise, a notion challenged by the Head Priest of the Izumo Shrine, Senge Takatomi, during the Pantheon Dispute.<ref name=":0">https://books.google.ca/books?id=ybljDQAAQBAJ&pg=PA380&lpg=PA380&dq=While+the+shrine+priests+struggled+to+establish+these+churches+in+order+to+support+their+proselytizing+as+Instructors,+however,+the+absence+of+public+funding+revealed+a+contradiction+with+the+rhetoric+of+shrines+as+kokka+no+s%C5%8Dshi,+public+facilities+providing+the+nation%E2%80%99s+rites+and+creed.+If+the+funding+had+to+be+arranged+entirely+on+a+private+basis,+then+what+foundation+was+there+for+claiming+that+shrines+had+a+public+status?+The+Shinto+Office+(Shint%C5%8D+jimukyoku)+was+supposed+to+smooth+these+bumps+in+the+road,+but+the+appointment+of+the+Ise+Shrines%E2%80%99+Chief+Priest+as+its+head+implied+that+Shinto+as+a+whole+should+center+on+Ise.+The+Head+Priest+of+the+Izumo+Shrine,+Senge+Takatomi+(1845%E2%80%931918),+challenged+that+idea+in+the+Pantheon+Dispute.&source=bl&ots=ePJp6y1JXS&sig=ACfU3U1vvWlDLshv_64QypQr5vdEAXk_yA&hl=en&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwiV-uf164yHAxVGn44IHXk1DcsQ6AF6BAgLEAM#v=onepage&q=While%20the%20shrine%20priests%20struggled%20to%20establish%20these%20churches%20in%20order%20to%20support%20their%20proselytizing%20as%20Instructors%2C%20however%2C%20the%20absence%20of%20public%20funding%20revealed%20a%20contradiction%20with%20the%20rhetoric%20of%20shrines%20as%20kokka%20no%20s%C5%8Dshi%2C%20public%20facilities%20providing%20the%20nation%E2%80%99s%20rites%20and%20creed.%20If%20the%20funding%20had%20to%20be%20arranged%20entirely%20on%20a%20private%20basis%2C%20then%20what%20foundation%20was%20there%20for%20claiming%20that%20shrines%20had%20a%20public%20status%3F%20The%20Shinto%20Office%20(Shint%C5%8D%20jimukyoku)%20was%20supposed%20to%20smooth%20these%20bumps%20in%20the%20road%2C%20but%20the%20appointment%20of%20the%20Ise%20Shrines%E2%80%99%20Chief%20Priest%20as%20its%20head%20implied%20that%20Shinto%20as%20a%20whole%20should%20center%20on%20Ise.%20The%20Head%20Priest%20of%20the%20Izumo%20Shrine%2C%20Senge%20Takatomi%20(1845%E2%80%931918)%2C%20challenged%20that%20idea%20in%20the%20Pantheon%20Dispute.&f=false</ref>
== references == <references />{{State Shinto}}

[[ja:神社教会]] '''Shrine Churches (神社教会, jinja kyōkai)''' were a kind of Shinto organization that existed in the [[Meiji period]]. They emerged as a significant organizational model in Japan during the Meiji period, influenced by the interplay between Buddhist and Shinto clergy and the policies of the [[Great Promulgation Campaign]].<ref name=":0" />
== Early Collaboration and Withdrawal == In the early years of the Great Promulgation Campaign, Buddhist and Shinto clergy worked together to promote national religious unity. However, this cooperation ended in 1875 when Buddhist sects began to withdraw. Shimaji Mokurai, an influential Shinshū priest, played a key role in this shift. After traveling to Europe, Mokurai became convinced that the state should remain separate from religion and tolerate diverse religious beliefs. His persistent petitions led to the Shinshū sect’s withdrawal from the Campaign. Similarly, in 1876, the Kurozumikyō and Shintō Shūseiha movements also received permission to withdraw. These withdrawals caused resentment among Campaign leaders, who viewed these sects as undermining Shinto’s image of unity.<ref name=":0" />
== Shift to Shinto Dominance == With the departure of the Buddhists, the Campaign became distinctly "Shinto" in character. In response, the government established the Shinto Office (神道事務局, Shintō jimukyoku) in 1875, headed by the Chief Priest of the Ise Shrines, to replace the Great Teaching Institute. This marked the beginning of state management over Shinto shrines, contrasting with the autonomous operation of Buddhist and other religious organizations outside the Campaign.<ref name=":0" />
== Financial Challenges and Formation of Shrine Churches == By 1876, there were over 10,000 National Instructors, but the lack of public funding posed significant challenges. The central government’s decision not to provide financial support led to the development of a new type of Shinto organization: the Shrine Church (教会, kyōkai). These churches were attached to shrines and organized by territory-based parishes, independently funded by their members. Priest-Instructors led these churches, promoting Shinto funerals and ancestral rites to parishioners, thus reducing reliance on Buddhist temples. However, performing funerals was problematic for Shinto priests due to the pollution of death, which conflicted with Shinto purity laws. By serving as Instructors, priests could separate their funeral duties from their shrine roles, alleviating this issue.<ref name=":0" />
== Jingūkyōkai and Its Expansion == The Ise Grand Shrines played a crucial role in this new organizational model by establishing Jingūkyōkai in 1872. Organized by Urata Nagatami and Tanaka Yoritsune, Jingūkyōkai included ministers from former pilgrim masters who also became Instructors. By 1875, Jingūkyōkai had 304,704 members and operated a network of 1,600 preaching stations nationwide, often located in existing shrines. The organization presented Amaterasu as an agricultural deity and distributed talismans and almanacs from the Ise Grand Shrines. Jingūkyōkai ministers frequently addressed large audiences, numbering in the thousands.<ref name=":0" />
== Ideological and Financial Contradictions == Despite these efforts, the absence of public funding revealed contradictions in the rhetoric of shrines as public institutions providing national rites and creeds. The appointment of the Ise Shrines’ Chief Priest to head the Shinto Office implied that Shinto should center on Ise, a notion challenged by the Head Priest of the Izumo Shrine, Senge Takatomi, during the Pantheon Dispute.<ref name=":0">https://books.google.ca/books?id=ybljDQAAQBAJ&pg=PA380&lpg=PA380&dq=While+the+shrine+priests+struggled+to+establish+these+churches+in+order+to+support+their+proselytizing+as+Instructors,+however,+the+absence+of+public+funding+revealed+a+contradiction+with+the+rhetoric+of+shrines+as+kokka+no+s%C5%8Dshi,+public+facilities+providing+the+nation%E2%80%99s+rites+and+creed.+If+the+funding+had+to+be+arranged+entirely+on+a+private+basis,+then+what+foundation+was+there+for+claiming+that+shrines+had+a+public+status?+The+Shinto+Office+(Shint%C5%8D+jimukyoku)+was+supposed+to+smooth+these+bumps+in+the+road,+but+the+appointment+of+the+Ise+Shrines%E2%80%99+Chief+Priest+as+its+head+implied+that+Shinto+as+a+whole+should+center+on+Ise.+The+Head+Priest+of+the+Izumo+Shrine,+Senge+Takatomi+(1845%E2%80%931918),+challenged+that+idea+in+the+Pantheon+Dispute.&source=bl&ots=ePJp6y1JXS&sig=ACfU3U1vvWlDLshv_64QypQr5vdEAXk_yA&hl=en&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwiV-uf164yHAxVGn44IHXk1DcsQ6AF6BAgLEAM#v=onepage&q=While%20the%20shrine%20priests%20struggled%20to%20establish%20these%20churches%20in%20order%20to%20support%20their%20proselytizing%20as%20Instructors%2C%20however%2C%20the%20absence%20of%20public%20funding%20revealed%20a%20contradiction%20with%20the%20rhetoric%20of%20shrines%20as%20kokka%20no%20s%C5%8Dshi%2C%20public%20facilities%20providing%20the%20nation%E2%80%99s%20rites%20and%20creed.%20If%20the%20funding%20had%20to%20be%20arranged%20entirely%20on%20a%20private%20basis%2C%20then%20what%20foundation%20was%20there%20for%20claiming%20that%20shrines%20had%20a%20public%20status%3F%20The%20Shinto%20Office%20(Shint%C5%8D%20jimukyoku)%20was%20supposed%20to%20smooth%20these%20bumps%20in%20the%20road%2C%20but%20the%20appointment%20of%20the%20Ise%20Shrines%E2%80%99%20Chief%20Priest%20as%20its%20head%20implied%20that%20Shinto%20as%20a%20whole%20should%20center%20on%20Ise.%20The%20Head%20Priest%20of%20the%20Izumo%20Shrine%2C%20Senge%20Takatomi%20(1845%E2%80%931918)%2C%20challenged%20that%20idea%20in%20the%20Pantheon%20Dispute.&f=false</ref>

[[ja:神社教会]] '''Shrine Churches (神社教会, jinja kyōkai)''' were a kind of Shinto organization that existed in the [[Meiji period]]. They emerged as a significant organizational model in Japan during the Meiji period, influenced by the interplay between Buddhist and Shinto clergy and the policies of the [[Great Promulgation Campaign]].<ref name=":0" />
== Early Collaboration and Withdrawal == In the early years of the Great Promulgation Campaign, Buddhist and Shinto clergy worked together to promote national religious unity. However, this cooperation ended in 1875 when Buddhist sects began to withdraw. Shimaji Mokurai, an influential Shinshū priest, played a key role in this shift. After traveling to Europe, Mokurai became convinced that the state should remain separate from religion and tolerate diverse religious beliefs. His persistent petitions led to the Shinshū sect’s withdrawal from the Campaign. Similarly, in 1876, the Kurozumikyō and Shintō Shūseiha movements also received permission to withdraw. These withdrawals caused resentment among Campaign leaders, who viewed these sects as undermining Shinto’s image of unity.<ref name=":0" />
== Shift to Shinto Dominance == With the departure of the Buddhists, the Campaign became distinctly "Shinto" in character. In response, the government established the Shinto Office (神道事務局, Shintō jimukyoku) in 1875, headed by the Chief Priest of the Ise Shrines, to replace the Great Teaching Institute. This marked the beginning of state management over Shinto shrines, contrasting with the autonomous operation of Buddhist and other religious organizations outside the Campaign.<ref name=":0" />
== Financial Challenges and Formation of Shrine Churches == By 1876, there were over 10,000 National Instructors, but the lack of public funding posed significant challenges. The central government’s decision not to provide financial support led to the development of a new type of Shinto organization: the Shrine Church (教会, kyōkai). These churches were attached to shrines and organized by territory-based parishes, independently funded by their members. Priest-Instructors led these churches, promoting Shinto funerals and ancestral rites to parishioners, thus reducing reliance on Buddhist temples. However, performing funerals was problematic for Shinto priests due to the pollution of death, which conflicted with Shinto purity laws. By serving as Instructors, priests could separate their funeral duties from their shrine roles, alleviating this issue.<ref name=":0" />
== Jingūkyōkai and Its Expansion == The Ise Grand Shrines played a crucial role in this new organizational model by establishing Jingūkyōkai in 1872. Organized by Urata Nagatami and Tanaka Yoritsune, Jingūkyōkai included ministers from former pilgrim masters who also became Instructors. By 1875, Jingūkyōkai had 304,704 members and operated a network of 1,600 preaching stations nationwide, often located in existing shrines. The organization presented Amaterasu as an agricultural deity and distributed talismans and almanacs from the Ise Grand Shrines. Jingūkyōkai ministers frequently addressed large audiences, numbering in the thousands.<ref name=":0" />
== Ideological and Financial Contradictions == Despite these efforts, the absence of public funding revealed contradictions in the rhetoric of shrines as public institutions providing national rites and creeds. The appointment of the Ise Shrines’ Chief Priest to head the Shinto Office implied that Shinto should center on Ise, a notion challenged by the Head Priest of the Izumo Shrine, Senge Takatomi, during the Pantheon Dispute.<ref name=":0">https://books.google.ca/books?id=ybljDQAAQBAJ&pg=PA380&lpg=PA380&dq=While+the+shrine+priests+struggled+to+establish+these+churches+in+order+to+support+their+proselytizing+as+Instructors,+however,+the+absence+of+public+funding+revealed+a+contradiction+with+the+rhetoric+of+shrines+as+kokka+no+s%C5%8Dshi,+public+facilities+providing+the+nation%E2%80%99s+rites+and+creed.+If+the+funding+had+to+be+arranged+entirely+on+a+private+basis,+then+what+foundation+was+there+for+claiming+that+shrines+had+a+public+status?+The+Shinto+Office+(Shint%C5%8D+jimukyoku)+was+supposed+to+smooth+these+bumps+in+the+road,+but+the+appointment+of+the+Ise+Shrines%E2%80%99+Chief+Priest+as+its+head+implied+that+Shinto+as+a+whole+should+center+on+Ise.+The+Head+Priest+of+the+Izumo+Shrine,+Senge+Takatomi+(1845%E2%80%931918),+challenged+that+idea+in+the+Pantheon+Dispute.&source=bl&ots=ePJp6y1JXS&sig=ACfU3U1vvWlDLshv_64QypQr5vdEAXk_yA&hl=en&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwiV-uf164yHAxVGn44IHXk1DcsQ6AF6BAgLEAM#v=onepage&q=While%20the%20shrine%20priests%20struggled%20to%20establish%20these%20churches%20in%20order%20to%20support%20their%20proselytizing%20as%20Instructors%2C%20however%2C%20the%20absence%20of%20public%20funding%20revealed%20a%20contradiction%20with%20the%20rhetoric%20of%20shrines%20as%20kokka%20no%20s%C5%8Dshi%2C%20public%20facilities%20providing%20the%20nation%E2%80%99s%20rites%20and%20creed.%20If%20the%20funding%20had%20to%20be%20arranged%20entirely%20on%20a%20private%20basis%2C%20then%20what%20foundation%20was%20there%20for%20claiming%20that%20shrines%20had%20a%20public%20status%3F%20The%20Shinto%20Office%20(Shint%C5%8D%20jimukyoku)%20was%20supposed%20to%20smooth%20these%20bumps%20in%20the%20road%2C%20but%20the%20appointment%20of%20the%20Ise%20Shrines%E2%80%99%20Chief%20Priest%20as%20its%20head%20implied%20that%20Shinto%20as%20a%20whole%20should%20center%20on%20Ise.%20The%20Head%20Priest%20of%20the%20Izumo%20Shrine%2C%20Senge%20Takatomi%20(1845%E2%80%931918)%2C%20challenged%20that%20idea%20in%20the%20Pantheon%20Dispute.&f=false</ref>

the Pantheon Dispute.<ref [[ja:神社教会]] '''Shrine Churches (神社教会, jinja kyōkai)''' were a kind of Shinto organization that existed in the [[Meiji period]]. They emerged as a significant organizational model in Japan during the Meiji period, influenced by the interplay between Buddhist and Shinto clergy and the policies of the [[Great Promulgation Campaign]].<ref name=":0" />
== Early Collaboration and Withdrawal == In the early years of the Great Promulgation Campaign, Buddhist and Shinto clergy worked together to promote national religious unity. However, this cooperation ended in 1875 when Buddhist sects began to withdraw. Shimaji Mokurai, an influential Shinshū priest, played a key role in this shift. After traveling to Europe, Mokurai became convinced that the state should remain separate from religion and tolerate diverse religious beliefs. His persistent petitions led to the Shinshū sect’s withdrawal from the Campaign. Similarly, in 1876, the Kurozumikyō and Shintō Shūseiha movements also received permission to withdraw. These withdrawals caused resentment among Campaign leaders, who viewed these sects as undermining Shinto’s image of unity.<ref name=":0" />
== Shift to Shinto Dominance == With the departure of the Buddhists, the Campaign became distinctly "Shinto" in character. In response, the government established the Shinto Office (神道事務局, Shintō jimukyoku) in 1875, headed by the Chief Priest of the Ise Shrines, to replace the Great Teaching Institute. This marked the beginning of state management over Shinto shrines, contrasting with the autonomous operation of Buddhist and other religious organizations outside the Campaign.<ref name=":0" />
== Financial Challenges and Formation of Shrine Churches == By 1876, there were over 10,000 National Instructors, but the lack of public funding posed significant challenges. The central government’s decision not to provide financial support led to the development of a new type of Shinto organization: the Shrine Church (教会, kyōkai). These churches were attached to shrines and organized by territory-based parishes, independently funded by their members. Priest-Instructors led these churches, promoting Shinto funerals and ancestral rites to parishioners, thus reducing reliance on Buddhist temples. However, performing funerals was problematic for Shinto priests due to the pollution of death, which conflicted with Shinto purity laws. By serving as Instructors, priests could separate their funeral duties from their shrine roles, alleviating this issue.<ref name=":0" />
== Jingūkyōkai and Its Expansion == The Ise Grand Shrines played a crucial role in this new organizational model by establishing Jingūkyōkai in 1872. Organized by Urata Nagatami and Tanaka Yoritsune, Jingūkyōkai included ministers from former pilgrim masters who also became Instructors. By 1875, Jingūkyōkai had 304,704 members and operated a network of 1,600 preaching stations nationwide, often located in existing shrines. The organization presented Amaterasu as an agricultural deity and distributed talismans and almanacs from the Ise Grand Shrines. Jingūkyōkai ministers frequently addressed large audiences, numbering in the thousands.<ref name=":0" />
== Ideological and Financial Contradictions == Despite these efforts, the absence of public funding revealed contradictions in the rhetoric of shrines as public institutions providing national rites and creeds. The appointment of the Ise Shrines’ Chief Priest to head the Shinto Office implied that Shinto should center on Ise, a notion challenged by the Head Priest of the Izumo Shrine, Senge Takatomi, during the Pantheon Dispute.<ref name=":0">https://books.google.ca/books?id=ybljDQAAQBAJ&pg=PA380&lpg=PA380&dq=While+the+shrine+priests+struggled+to+establish+these+churches+in+order+to+support+their+proselytizing+as+Instructors,+however,+the+absence+of+public+funding+revealed+a+contradiction+with+the+rhetoric+of+shrines+as+kokka+no+s%C5%8Dshi,+public+facilities+providing+the+nation%E2%80%99s+rites+and+creed.+If+the+funding+had+to+be+arranged+entirely+on+a+private+basis,+then+what+foundation+was+there+for+claiming+that+shrines+had+a+public+status?+The+Shinto+Office+(Shint%C5%8D+jimukyoku)+was+supposed+to+smooth+these+bumps+in+the+road,+but+the+appointment+of+the+Ise+Shrines%E2%80%99+Chief+Priest+as+its+head+implied+that+Shinto+as+a+whole+should+center+on+Ise.+The+Head+Priest+of+the+Izumo+Shrine,+Senge+Takatomi+(1845%E2%80%931918),+challenged+that+idea+in+the+Pantheon+Dispute.&source=bl&ots=ePJp6y1JXS&sig=ACfU3U1vvWlDLshv_64QypQr5vdEAXk_yA&hl=en&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwiV-uf164yHAxVGn44IHXk1DcsQ6AF6BAgLEAM#v=onepage&q=While%20the%20shrine%20priests%20struggled%20to%20establish%20these%20churches%20in%20order%20to%20support%20their%20proselytizing%20as%20Instructors%2C%20however%2C%20the%20absence%20of%20public%20funding%20revealed%20a%20contradiction%20with%20the%20rhetoric%20of%20shrines%20as%20kokka%20no%20s%C5%8Dshi%2C%20public%20facilities%20providing%20the%20nation%E2%80%99s%20rites%20and%20creed.%20If%20the%20funding%20had%20to%20be%20arranged%20entirely%20on%20a%20private%20basis%2C%20then%20what%20foundation%20was%20there%20for%20claiming%20that%20shrines%20had%20a%20public%20status%3F%20The%20Shinto%20Office%20(Shint%C5%8D%20jimukyoku)%20was%20supposed%20to%20smooth%20these%20bumps%20in%20the%20road%2C%20but%20the%20appointment%20of%20the%20Ise%20Shrines%E2%80%99%20Chief%20Priest%20as%20its%20head%20implied%20that%20Shinto%20as%20a%20whole%20should%20center%20on%20Ise.%20The%20Head%20Priest%20of%20the%20Izumo%20Shrine%2C%20Senge%20Takatomi%20(1845%E2%80%931918)%2C%20challenged%20that%20idea%20in%20the%20Pantheon%20Dispute.&f=false</ref>

[[ja:神社教会]] '''Shrine Churches (神社教会, jinja kyōkai)''' were a kind of Shinto organization that existed in the [[Meiji period]]. They emerged as a significant organizational model in Japan during the Meiji period, influenced by the interplay between Buddhist and Shinto clergy and the policies of the [[Great Promulgation Campaign]].<ref name=":0" />
== Early Collaboration and Withdrawal == In the early years of the Great Promulgation Campaign, Buddhist and Shinto clergy worked together to promote national religious unity. However, this cooperation ended in 1875 when Buddhist sects began to withdraw. Shimaji Mokurai, an influential Shinshū priest, played a key role in this shift. After traveling to Europe, Mokurai became convinced that the state should remain separate from religion and tolerate diverse religious beliefs. His persistent petitions led to the Shinshū sect’s withdrawal from the Campaign. Similarly, in 1876, the Kurozumikyō and Shintō Shūseiha movements also received permission to withdraw. These withdrawals caused resentment among Campaign leaders, who viewed these sects as undermining Shinto’s image of unity.<ref name=":0" />
== Shift to Shinto Dominance == With the departure of the Buddhists, the Campaign became distinctly "Shinto" in character. In response, the government established the Shinto Office (神道事務局, Shintō jimukyoku) in 1875, headed by the Chief Priest of the Ise Shrines, to replace the Great Teaching Institute. This marked the beginning of state management over Shinto shrines, contrasting with the autonomous operation of Buddhist and other religious organizations outside the Campaign.<ref name=":0" />
== Financial Challenges and Formation of Shrine Churches == By 1876, there were over 10,000 National Instructors, but the lack of public funding posed significant challenges. The central government’s decision not to provide financial support led to the development of a new type of Shinto organization: the Shrine Church (教会, kyōkai). These churches were attached to shrines and organized by territory-based parishes, independently funded by their members. Priest-Instructors led these churches, promoting Shinto funerals and ancestral rites to parishioners, thus reducing reliance on Buddhist temples. However, performing funerals was problematic for Shinto priests due to the pollution of death, which conflicted with Shinto purity laws. By serving as Instructors, priests could separate their funeral duties from their shrine roles, alleviating this issue.<ref name=":0" />
== Jingūkyōkai and Its Expansion == The Ise Grand Shrines played a crucial role in this new organizational model by establishing Jingūkyōkai in 1872. Organized by Urata Nagatami and Tanaka Yoritsune, Jingūkyōkai included ministers from former pilgrim masters who also became Instructors. By 1875, Jingūkyōkai had 304,704 members and operated a network of 1,600 preaching stations nationwide, often located in existing shrines. The organization presented Amaterasu as an agricultural deity and distributed talismans and almanacs from the Ise Grand Shrines. Jingūkyōkai ministers frequently addressed large audiences, numbering in the thousands.<ref name=":0" />
== Ideological and Financial Contradictions == Despite these efforts, the absence of public funding revealed contradictions in the rhetoric of shrines as public institutions providing national rites and creeds. The appointment of the Ise Shrines’ Chief Priest to head the Shinto Office implied that Shinto should center on Ise, a notion challenged by the Head Priest of the Izumo Shrine, Senge Takatomi, during the Pantheon Dispute.<ref name=":0">https://books.google.ca/books?id=ybljDQAAQBAJ&pg=PA380&lpg=PA380&dq=While+the+shrine+priests+struggled+to+establish+these+churches+in+order+to+support+their+proselytizing+as+Instructors,+however,+the+absence+of+public+funding+revealed+a+contradiction+with+the+rhetoric+of+shrines+as+kokka+no+s%C5%8Dshi,+public+facilities+providing+the+nation%E2%80%99s+rites+and+creed.+If+the+funding+had+to+be+arranged+entirely+on+a+private+basis,+then+what+foundation+was+there+for+claiming+that+shrines+had+a+public+status?+The+Shinto+Office+(Shint%C5%8D+jimukyoku)+was+supposed+to+smooth+these+bumps+in+the+road,+but+the+appointment+of+the+Ise+Shrines%E2%80%99+Chief+Priest+as+its+head+implied+that+Shinto+as+a+whole+should+center+on+Ise.+The+Head+Priest+of+the+Izumo+Shrine,+Senge+Takatomi+(1845%E2%80%931918),+challenged+that+idea+in+the+Pantheon+Dispute.&source=bl&ots=ePJp6y1JXS&sig=ACfU3U1vvWlDLshv_64QypQr5vdEAXk_yA&hl=en&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwiV-uf164yHAxVGn44IHXk1DcsQ6AF6BAgLEAM#v=onepage&q=While%20the%20shrine%20priests%20struggled%20to%20establish%20these%20churches%20in%20order%20to%20support%20their%20proselytizing%20as%20Instructors%2C%20however%2C%20the%20absence%20of%20public%20funding%20revealed%20a%20contradiction%20with%20the%20rhetoric%20of%20shrines%20as%20kokka%20no%20s%C5%8Dshi%2C%20public%20facilities%20providing%20the%20nation%E2%80%99s%20rites%20and%20creed.%20If%20the%20funding%20had%20to%20be%20arranged%20entirely%20on%20a%20private%20basis%2C%20then%20what%20foundation%20was%20there%20for%20claiming%20that%20shrines%20had%20a%20public%20status%3F%20The%20Shinto%20Office%20(Shint%C5%8D%20jimukyoku)%20was%20supposed%20to%20smooth%20these%20bumps%20in%20the%20road%2C%20but%20the%20appointment%20of%20the%20Ise%20Shrines%E2%80%99%20Chief%20Priest%20as%20its%20head%20implied%20that%20Shinto%20as%20a%20whole%20should%20center%20on%20Ise.%20The%20Head%20Priest%20of%20the%20Izumo%20Shrine%2C%20Senge%20Takatomi%20(1845%E2%80%931918)%2C%20challenged%20that%20idea%20in%20the%20Pantheon%20Dispute.&f=false</ref>

Organized by Urata Nagatami and Tanaka Yoritsune, Jingūkyōkai included ministers from former pilgrim masters who also became Instructors. By 1875, Jingūkyōkai had 304,704 members and operated a network of 1,600 preaching stations nationwide, often located in existing shrines. The organization presented Amaterasu as an agricultural deity and distributed talismans and almanacs from the Ise Grand Shrines. Jingūkyōkai ministers frequently addressed large audiences, numbering in the thousands.<ref name=":0" />
== Ideological and Financial Contradictions == Despite these efforts, the absence of public funding revealed contradictions in the rhetoric of shrines as public institutions providing national rites and creeds. The appointment of the Ise Shrines’ Chief Priest to head the Shinto Office implied that Shinto should center on Ise, a notion challenged by the Head Priest of the Izumo Shrine, Senge Takatomi, during the Pantheon Dispute.<ref name=":0">https://books.google.ca/books?id=ybljDQAAQBAJ&pg=PA380&lpg=PA380&dq=While+the+shrine+priests+struggled+to+establish+these+churches+in+order+to+support+their+proselytizing+as+Instructors,+however,+the+absence+of+public+funding+revealed+a+contradiction+with+the+rhetoric+of+shrines+as+kokka+no+s%C5%8Dshi,+public+facilities+providing+the+nation%E2%80%99s+rites+and+creed.+If+the+funding+had+to+be+arranged+entirely+on+a+private+basis,+then+what+foundation+was+there+for+claiming+that+shrines+had+a+public+status?+The+Shinto+Office+(Shint%C5%8D+jimukyoku)+was+supposed+to+smooth+these+bumps+in+the+road,+but+the+appointment+of+the+Ise+Shrines%E2%80%99+Chief+Priest+as+its+head+implied+that+Shinto+as+a+whole+should+center+on+Ise.+The+Head+Priest+of+the+Izumo+Shrine,+Senge+Takatomi+(1845%E2%80%931918),+challenged+that+idea+in+the+Pantheon+Dispute.&source=bl&ots=ePJp6y1JXS&sig=ACfU3U1vvWlDLshv_64QypQr5vdEAXk_yA&hl=en&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwiV-uf164yHAxVGn44IHXk1DcsQ6AF6BAgLEAM#v=onepage&q=While%20the%20shrine%20priests%20struggled%20to%20establish%20these%20churches%20in%20order%20to%20support%20their%20proselytizing%20as%20Instructors%2C%20however%2C%20the%20absence%20of%20public%20funding%20revealed%20a%20contradiction%20with%20the%20rhetoric%20of%20shrines%20as%20kokka%20no%20s%C5%8Dshi%2C%20public%20facilities%20providing%20the%20nation%E2%80%99s%20rites%20and%20creed.%20If%20the%20funding%20had%20to%20be%20arranged%20entirely%20on%20a%20private%20basis%2C%20then%20what%20foundation%20was%20there%20for%20claiming%20that%20shrines%20had%20a%20public%20status%3F%20The%20Shinto%20Office%20(Shint%C5%8D%20jimukyoku)%20was%20supposed%20to%20smooth%20these%20bumps%20in%20the%20road%2C%20but%20the%20appointment%20of%20the%20Ise%20Shrines%E2%80%99%20Chief%20Priest%20as%20its%20head%20implied%20that%20Shinto%20as%20a%20whole%20should%20center%20on%20Ise.%20The%20Head%20Priest%20of%20the%20Izumo%20Shrine%2C%20Senge%20Takatomi%20(1845%E2%80%931918)%2C%20challenged%20that%20idea%20in%20 [[ja:神社教会]] '''Shrine Churches (神社教会, jinja kyōkai)''' were a kind of Shinto organization that existed in the [[Meiji period]]. They emerged as a significant organizational model in Japan during the Meiji period, influenced by the interplay between Buddhist and Shinto clergy and the policies of the [[Great Promulgation Campaign]].<ref name=":0" />
== Early Collaboration and Withdrawal == In the early years of the Great Promulgation Campaign, Buddhist and Shinto clergy worked together to promote national religious unity. However, this cooperation ended in 1875 when Buddhist sects began to withdraw. Shimaji Mokurai, an influential Shinshū priest, played a key role in this shift. After traveling to Europe, Mokurai became convinced that the state should remain separate from religion and tolerate diverse religious beliefs. His persistent petitions led to the Shinshū sect’s withdrawal from the Campaign. Similarly, in 1876, the Kurozumikyō and Shintō Shūseiha movements also received permission to withdraw. These withdrawals caused resentment among Campaign leaders, who viewed these sects as undermining Shinto’s image of unity.<ref name=":0" />
== Shift to Shinto Dominance == With the departure of the Buddhists, the Campaign became distinctly "Shinto" in character. In response, the government established the Shinto Office (神道事務局, Shintō jimukyoku) in 1875, headed by the Chief Priest of the Ise Shrines, to replace the Great Teaching Institute. This marked the beginning of state management over Shinto shrines, contrasting with the autonomous operation of Buddhist and other religious organizations outside the Campaign.<ref name=":0" />
== Financial Challenges and Formation of Shrine Churches == By 1876, there were over 10,000 National Instructors, but the lack of public funding posed significant challenges. The central government’s decision not to provide financial support led to the development of a new type of Shinto organization: the Shrine Church (教会, kyōkai). These churches were attached to shrines and organized by territory-based parishes, independently funded by their members. Priest-Instructors led these churches, promoting Shinto funerals and ancestral rites to parishioners, thus reducing reliance on Buddhist temples. However, performing funerals was problematic for Shinto priests due to the pollution of death, which conflicted with Shinto purity laws. By serving as Instructors, priests could separate their funeral duties from their shrine roles, alleviating this issue.<ref name=":0" />
== Jingūkyōkai and Its Expansion == The Ise Grand Shrines played a crucial role in this new organizational model by establishing Jingūkyōkai in 1872. Organized by Urata Nagatami and Tanaka Yoritsune, Jingūkyōkai included ministers from former pilgrim masters who also became Instructors. By 1875, Jingūkyōkai had 304,704 members and operated a network of 1,600 preaching stations nationwide, often located in existing shrines. The organization presented Amaterasu as an agricultural deity and distributed talismans and almanacs from the Ise Grand Shrines. Jingūkyōkai ministers frequently addressed large audiences, numbering in the thousands.<ref name=":0" />
== Ideological and Financial Contradictions == Despite these efforts, the absence of public funding revealed contradictions in the rhetoric of shrines as public institutions providing national rites and creeds. The appointment of the Ise Shrines’ Chief Priest to head the Shinto Office implied that Shinto should center on Ise, a notion challenged by the Head Priest of the Izumo Shrine, Senge Takatomi, during the Pantheon Dispute.<ref name=":0">https://books.google.ca/books?id=ybljDQAAQBAJ&pg=PA380&lpg=PA380&dq=While+the+shrine+priests+struggled+to+establish+these+churches+in+order+to+support+their+proselytizing+as+Instructors,+however,+the+absence+of+public+funding+revealed+a+contradiction+with+the+rhetoric+of+shrines+as+kokka+no+s%C5%8Dshi,+public+facilities+providing+the+nation%E2%80%99s+rites+and+creed.+If+the+funding+had+to+be+arranged+entirely+on+a+private+basis,+then+what+foundation+was+there+for+claiming+that+shrines+had+a+public+status?+The+Shinto+Office+(Shint%C5%8D+jimukyoku)+was+supposed+to+smooth+these+bumps+in+the+road,+but+the+appointment+of+the+Ise+Shrines%E2%80%99+Chief+Priest+as+its+head+implied+that+Shinto+as+a+whole+should+center+on+Ise.+The+Head+Priest+of+the+Izumo+Shrine,+Senge+Takatomi+(1845%E2%80%931918),+challenged+that+idea+in+the+Pantheon+Dispute.&source=bl&ots=ePJp6y1JXS&sig=ACfU3U1vvWlDLshv_64QypQr5vdEAXk_yA&hl=en&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwiV-uf164yHAxVGn44IHXk1DcsQ6AF6BAgLEAM#v=onepage&q=While%20the%20shrine%20priests%20struggled%20to%20establish%20these%20churches%20in%20order%20to%20support%20their%20proselytizing%20as%20Instructors%2C%20however%2C%20the%20absence%20of%20public%20funding%20revealed%20a%20contradiction%20with%20the%20rhetoric%20of%20shrines%20as%20kokka%20no%20s%C5%8Dshi%2C%20public%20facilities%20providing%20the%20nation%E2%80%99s%20rites%20and%20creed.%20If%20the%20funding%20had%20to%20be%20arranged%20entirely%20on%20a%20private%20basis%2C%20then%20what%20foundation%20was%20there%20for%20claiming%20that%20shrines%20had%20a%20public%20status%3F%20The%20Shinto%20Office%20(Shint%C5%8D%20jimukyoku)%20was%20supposed%20to%20smooth%20these%20bumps%20in%20the%20road%2C%20but%20the%20appointment%20of%20the%20Ise%20Shrines%E2%80%99%20Chief%20Priest%20as%20its%20head%20implied%20that%20Shinto%20as%20a%20whole%20should%20center%20on%20Ise.%20The%20Head%20Priest%20of%20the%20Izumo%20Shrine%2C%20Senge%20Takatomi%20(1845%E2%80%931918)%2C%20challenged%20that%20idea%20in%20the%20Pantheon%20Dispute.&f=false</ref>

[[ja:神社教会]] '''Shrine Churches (神社教会, jinja kyōkai)''' were a kind of Shinto organization that existed in the [[Meiji period]]. They emerged as a significant organizational model in Japan during the Meiji period, influenced by the interplay between Buddhist and Shinto clergy and the policies of the [[Great Promulgation Campaign]].<ref name=":0" />
== Early Collaboration and Withdrawal == In the early years of the Great Promulgation Campaign, Buddhist and Shinto clergy worked together to promote national religious unity. However, this cooperation ended in 1875 when Buddhist sects began to withdraw. Shimaji Mokurai, an influential Shinshū priest, played a key role in this shift. After traveling to Europe, Mokurai became convinced that the state should remain separate from religion and tolerate diverse religious beliefs. His persistent petitions led to the Shinshū sect’s withdrawal from the Campaign. Similarly, in 1876, the Kurozumikyō and Shintō Shūseiha movements also received permission to withdraw. These withdrawals caused resentment among Campaign leaders, who viewed these sects as undermining Shinto’s image of unity.<ref name=":0" />
== Shift to Shinto Dominance == With the departure of the Buddhists, the Campaign became distinctly "Shinto" in character. In response, the government established the Shinto Office (神道事務局, Shintō jimukyoku) in 1875, headed by the Chief Priest of the Ise Shrines, to replace the Great Teaching Institute. This marked the beginning of state management over Shinto shrines, contrasting with the autonomous operation of Buddhist and other religious organizations outside the Campaign.<ref name=":0" />
== Financial Challenges and Formation of Shrine Churches == By 1876, there were over 10,000 National Instructors, but the lack of public funding posed significant challenges. The central government’s decision not to provide financial support led to the development of a new type of Shinto organization: the Shrine Church (教会, kyōkai). These churches were attached to shrines and organized by territory-based parishes, independently funded by their members. Priest-Instructors led these churches, promoting Shinto funerals and ancestral rites to parishioners, thus reducing reliance on Buddhist temples. However, performing funerals was problematic for Shinto priests due to the pollution of death, which conflicted with Shinto purity laws. By serving as Instructors, priests could separate their funeral duties from their shrine roles, alleviating this issue.<ref name=":0" />
== Jingūkyōkai and Its Expansion == The Ise Grand Shrines played a crucial role in this new organizational model by establishing Jingūkyōkai in 1872. Organized by Urata Nagatami and Tanaka Yoritsune, Jingūkyōkai included ministers from former pilgrim masters who also became Instructors. By 1875, Jingūkyōkai had 304,704 members and operated a network of 1,600 preaching stations nationwide, often located in existing shrines. The organization presented Amaterasu as an agricultural deity and distributed talismans and almanacs from the Ise Grand Shrines. Jingūkyōkai ministers frequently addressed large audiences, numbering in the thousands.<ref name=":0" />
== Ideological and Financial Contradictions == Despite these efforts, the absence of public funding revealed contradictions in the rhetoric of shrines as public institutions providing national rites and creeds. The appointment of the Ise Shrines’ Chief Priest to head the Shinto Office implied that Shinto should center on Ise, a notion challenged by the Head Priest of the Izumo Shrine, Senge Takatomi, during the Pantheon Dispute.<ref name=":0">https://books.google.ca/books?id=ybljDQAAQBAJ&pg=PA380&lpg=PA380&dq=While+the+shrine+priests+struggled+to+establish+these+churches+in+order+to+support+their+proselytizing+as+Instructors,+however,+the+absence+of+public+funding+revealed+a+contradiction+with+the+rhetoric+of+shrines+as+kokka+no+s%C5%8Dshi,+public+facilities+providing+the+nation%E2%80%99s+rites+and+creed.+If+the+funding+had+to+be+arranged+entirely+on+a+private+basis,+then+what+foundation+was+there+for+claiming+that+shrines+had+a+public+status?+The+Shinto+Office+(Shint%C5%8D+jimukyoku)+was+supposed+to+smooth+these+bumps+in+the+road,+but+the+appointment+of+the+Ise+Shrines%E2%80%99+Chief+Priest+as+its+head+implied+that+Shinto+as+a+whole+should+center+on+Ise.+The+Head+Priest+of+the+Izumo+Shrine,+Senge+Takatomi+(1845%E2%80%931918),+challenged+that+idea+in+the+Pantheon+Dispute.&source=bl&ots=ePJp6y1JXS&sig=ACfU3U1vvWlDLshv_64QypQr5vdEAXk_yA&hl=en&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwiV-uf164yHAxVGn44IHXk1DcsQ6AF6BAgLEAM#v=onepage&q=While%20the%20shrine%20priests%20struggled%20to%20establish%20these%20churches%20in%20order%20to%20support%20their%20proselytizing%20as%20Instructors%2C%20however%2C%20the%20absence%20of%20public%20funding%20revealed%20a%20contradiction%20with%20the%20rhetoric%20of%20shrines%20as%20kokka%20no%20s%C5%8Dshi%2C%20public%20facilities%20providing%20the%20nation%E2%80%99s%20rites%20and%20creed.%20If%20the%20funding%20had%20to%20be%20arranged%20entirely%20on%20a%20private%20basis%2C%20then%20what%20foundation%20was%20there%20for%20claiming%20that%20shrines%20had%20a%20public%20status%3F%20The%20Shinto%20Office%20(Shint%C5%8D%20jimukyoku)%20was%20supposed%20to%20smooth%20these%20bumps%20in%20the%20road%2C%20but%20the%20appointment%20of%20the%20Ise%20Shrines%E2%80%99%20Chief%20Priest%20as%20its%20head%20implied%20that%20Shinto%20as%20a%20whole%20should%20center%20on%20Ise.%20The%20Head%20Priest%20of%20the%20Izumo%20Shrine%2C%20Senge%20Takatomi%20(1845%E2%80%931918)%2C%20challenged%20that%20idea%20in%20the%20Pantheon%20Dispute.&f=false</ref>

[[ja:神社教会]] '''Shrine Churches (神社教会, jinja kyōkai)''' were a kind of Shinto organization that existed in the [[Meiji period]]. They emerged as a significant organizational model in Japan during the Meiji period, influenced by the interplay between Buddhist and Shinto clergy and the policies of the [[Great Promulgation Campaign]].<ref name=":0" />
== Early Collaboration and Withdrawal == In the early years of the Great Promulgation Campaign, Buddhist and Shinto clergy worked together to promote national religious unity. However, this cooperation ended in 1875 when Buddhist sects began to withdraw. Shimaji Mokurai, an influential Shinshū priest, played a key role in this shift. After traveling to Europe, Mokurai became convinced that the state should remain separate from religion and tolerate diverse religious beliefs. His persistent petitions led to the Shinshū sect’s withdrawal from the Campaign. Similarly, in 1876, the Kurozumikyō and Shintō Shūseiha movements also received permission to withdraw. These withdrawals caused resentment among Campaign leaders, who viewed these sects as undermining Shinto’s image of unity.<ref name=":0" />
== Shift to Shinto Dominance == With the departure of the Buddhists, the Campaign became distinctly "Shinto" in character. In response, the government established the Shinto Office (神道事務局, Shintō jimukyoku) in 1875, headed by the Chief Priest of the Ise Shrines, to replace the Great Teaching Institute. This marked the beginning of state management over Shinto shrines, contrasting with the autonomous operation of Buddhist and other religious organizations outside the Campaign.<ref name=":0" />
== Financial Challenges and Formation of Shrine Churches == By 1876, there were over 10,000 National Instructors, but the lack of public funding posed significant challenges. The central government’s decision not to provide financial support led to the development of a new type of Shinto organization: the Shrine Church (教会, kyōkai). These churches were attached to shrines and organized by territory-based parishes, independently funded by their members. Priest-Instructors led these churches, promoting Shinto funerals and ancestral rites to parishioners, thus reducing reliance on Buddhist temples. However, performing funerals was problematic for Shinto priests due to the pollution of death, which conflicted with Shinto purity laws. By serving as Instructors, priests could separate their funeral duties from their shrine roles, alleviating this issue.<ref name=":0" />
== Jingūkyōkai and Its Expansion == The Ise Grand Shrines played a crucial role in this new organizational model by establishing Jingūkyōkai in 1872. Organized by Urata Nagatami and Tanaka Yoritsune, Jingūkyōkai included ministers from former pilgrim masters who also became Instructors. By 1875, Jingūkyōkai had 304,704 members and operated a network of 1,600 preaching stations nationwide, often located in existing shrines. The organization presented Amaterasu as an agricultural deity and distributed talismans and almanacs from the Ise Grand Shrines. Jingūkyōkai ministers frequently addressed large audiences, numbering in the thousands.<ref name=":0" />
== Ideological and Financial Contradictions == Despite these efforts, the absence of public funding revealed contradictions in the rhetoric of shrines as public institutions providing national rites and creeds. The appointment of the Ise Shrines’ Chief Priest to head the Shinto Office implied that Shinto should center on Ise, a notion challenged by the Head Priest of the Izumo Shrine, Senge Takatomi, during the Pantheon Dispute.<ref name=":0">https://books.google.ca/books?id=ybljDQAAQBAJ&pg=PA380&lpg=PA380&dq=While+the+shrine+priests+struggled+to+establish+these+churches+in+order+to+support+their+proselytizing+as+Instructors,+however,+the+absence+of+public+funding+revealed+a+contradiction+with+the+rhetoric+of+shrines+as+kokka+no+s%C5%8Dshi,+public+facilities+providing+the+nation%E2%80%99s+rites+and+creed.+If+the+funding+had+to+be+arranged+entirely+on+a+private+basis,+then+what+foundation+was+there+for+claiming+that+shrines+had+a+public+status?+The+Shinto+Office+(Shint%C5%8D+jimukyoku)+was+supposed+to+smooth+these+bumps+in+the+road,+but+the+appointment+of+the+Ise+Shrines%E2%80%99+Chief+Priest+as+its+head+implied+that+Shinto+as+a+whole+should+center+on+Ise.+The+Head+Priest+of+the+Izumo+Shrine,+Senge+Takatomi+(1845%E2%80%931918),+challenged+that+idea+in+the+Pantheon+Dispute.&source=bl&ots=ePJp6y1JXS&sig=ACfU3U1vvWlDLshv_64QypQr5vdEAXk_yA&hl=en&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwiV-uf164yHAxVGn44IHXk1DcsQ6AF6BAgLEAM#v=onepage&q=While%20the%20shrine%20priests%20struggled%20to%20establish%20these%20churches%20in%20order%20to%20support%20their%20proselytizing%20as%20Instructors%2C%20however%2C%20the%20absence%20of%20public%20funding%20revealed%20a%20contradiction%20with%20the%20rhetoric%20of%20shrines%20as%20kokka%20no%20s%C5%8Dshi%2C%20public%20facilities%20providing%20the%20nation%E2%80%99s%20rites%20and%20creed.%20If%20the%20funding%20had%20to%20be%20arranged%20entirely%20on%20a%20private%20basis%2C%20then%20what%20foundation%20was%20there%20for%20claiming%20that%20shrines%20had%20a%20public%20status%3F%20The%20Shinto%20Office%20(Shint%C5%8D%20jimukyoku)%20was%20supposed%20to%20smooth%20these%20bumps%20in%20the%20road%2C%20but%20the%20appointment%20of%20the%20Ise%20Shrines%E2%80%99%20Chief%20Priest%20as%20its%20head%20implied%20that%20Shinto%20as%20a%20whole%20should%20center%20on%20Ise.%20The%20Head%20Priest%20of%20the%20Izumo%20Shrine%2C%20Senge%20Takatomi%20(1845%E2%80%931918)%2C%20challenged%20that%20idea%20in%20the%20Pantheon%20Dispute.&f=false</ref>

They emerged as a significant organizational model in Japan during the Meiji period, influenced by the interplay between Buddhist and Shinto clergy and the policies of the [[Great Promulgation Campaign]].<ref name=":0" />
== Early Collaboration and Withdrawal == In the early years of the Great Promulgation Campaign, Buddhist and Shinto clergy worked together to promote national religious unity. However, this cooperation ended in 1875 when Buddhist sects began to withdraw. Shimaji Mokurai, an influential Shinshū priest, played a key role in this shift. After traveling to Europe, Mokurai became convinced that the state should remain separate from religion and tolerate diverse religious beliefs. His persistent petitions led to the Shinshū sect’s withdrawal from the Campaign. Similarly, in 1876, the Kurozumikyō and Shintō Shūseiha movements also received permission to withdraw. These withdrawals caused resentment among Campaign leaders, who viewed these sects as undermining Shinto’s image of unity.<ref name=":0" />
== Shift to Shinto Dominance == With the departure of the Buddhists, the Campaign became distinctly "Shinto" in character. In response, the government established the Shinto Office (神道事務局, Shintō jimukyoku) in 1875, headed by the Chief Priest of the Ise Shrines, to replace the Great Teaching Institute. This marked the beginning of state management over Shinto shrines, contrasting with the autonomous operation of Buddhist and other religious organizations outside the Campaign.<ref name=":0" />

The central government’s decision not to provide financial support led to the development of a new type of Shinto organization: the Shrine Church (教会, kyōkai). These churches were attached to shrines and organized by territory-based parishes, independently funded by their members. Priest-Instructors led these churches, promoting Shinto funerals and ancestral rites to parishioners, thus reducing reliance on Buddhist temples. However, performing funerals was problematic for Shinto priests due to the pollution of death, which conflicted with Shinto purity laws. By serving as Instructors, priests could separate their funeral duties from their shrine roles, alleviating this issue.<ref name=":0" />

the Pantheon Dispute.<ref name=":0">https://books.google.ca/books?id=ybljDQAAQBAJ&pg=PA380&lpg=PA380&dq=While+the+shrine+priests+struggled+to+establish+these+churches+in+order+to+support+their+proselytizing+as+Instructors,+however,+the+absence+of+public+funding+revealed+a+contradiction+with+the+rhetoric+of+shrines+as+kokka+no+s%C5%8Dshi,+public+facilities+providing+the+nation%E2%80%99s+rites+and+creed.+If+the+funding+had+to+be+arranged+entirely+on+a+private+basis,+then+what+foundation+was+there+for+claiming+that+shrines+had+a+public+status?+The+Shinto+Office+(Shint%C5%8D+jimukyoku)+was+supposed+to+smooth+these+bumps+in+the+road,+but+the+appointment+of+the+Ise+Shrines%E2%80%99+Chief+Priest+as+its+head+implied+that+Shinto+as+a+whole+should+center+on+Ise.+The+Head+Priest+of+the+Izumo+Shrine,+Senge+Takatomi+(1845%E2%80%931918),+challenged+that+idea+in+the+Pantheon+Dispute.&source=bl&ots=ePJp6y1JXS&sig=ACfU3U1vvWlDLshv_64QypQr5vdEAXk_yA&hl=en&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwiV-uf164yHAxVGn44IHXk1DcsQ6AF6BAgLEAM#v=onepage&q=While%20the%20shrine%20priests%20struggled%20to%20establish%20these%20churches%20in%20order%20to%20support%20their%20proselytizing%20as%20Instructors%2C%20however%2C%20the%20absence%20of%20public%20funding%20revealed%20a%20contradiction%20with%20the%20rhetoric%20of%20shrines%20as%20kokka%20no%20s%C5%8Dshi%2C%20public%20facilities%20providing%20the%20nation%E2%80%99s%20rites%20and%20creed.%20If%20the%20funding%20had%20to%20be%20arranged%20entirely%20on%20a%20private%20basis%2C%20then%20what%20foundation%20was%20there%20for%20claiming%20that%20shrines%20had%20a%20public%20status%3F%20The%20Shinto%20Office%20(Shint%C5%8D%20jimukyoku)%20was%20supposed%20to%20smooth%20these%20bumps%20in%20the%20road%2C%20but%20the%20appointment%20of%20the%20Ise%20Shrines%E2%80%99%20Chief%20Priest%20as%20its%20head%20implied%20that%20Shinto%20as%20a%20whole%20should%20center%20on%20Ise.%20The%20Head%20Priest%20of%20the%20Izumo%20Shrine%2C%20Senge%20Takatomi%20(1845%E2%80%931918)%2C%20challenged%20that%20idea%20in%20 == Jingūkyōkai and Its Expansion == The Ise Grand Shrines played a crucial role in this new organizational model by establishing Jingūkyōkai in 1872. Organized by Urata Nagatami and Tanaka Yoritsune, Jingūkyōkai included ministers from former pilgrim masters who also became Instructors. By 1875, Jingūkyōkai had 304,704 members and operated a network of 1,600 preaching stations nationwide, often located in existing shrines. The organization presented Amaterasu as an agricultural deity and distributed talismans and almanacs from the Ise Grand Shrines. Jingūkyōkai ministers frequently addressed large audiences, numbering in the thousands.<ref name=":0" />
== Ideological and Financial Contradictions == Despite these efforts, the absence of public funding revealed contradictions in the rhetoric of shrines as public institutions providing national rites and creeds. The appointment of the Ise Shrines’ Chief Priest to head the Shinto Office implied that Shinto should center on Ise, a notion challenged by the Head Priest of the Izumo Shrine, Senge Takatomi, during the Pantheon Dispute.<ref name=":0">https://books.google.ca/books?id=ybljDQAAQBAJ&pg=PA380&lpg=PA380&dq=While+the+shrine+priests+struggled+to+establish+these+churches+in+order+to+support+their+proselytizing+as+Instructors,+however,+the+absence+of+public+funding+revealed+a+contradiction+with+the+rhetoric+of+shrines+as+kokka+no+s%C5%8Dshi,+public+facilities+providing+the+nation%E2%80%99s+rites+and+creed.+If+the+funding+had+to+be+arranged+entirely+on+a+private+basis,+then+what+foundation+was+there+for+claiming+that+shrines+had+a+public+status?+The+Shinto+Office+(Shint%C5%8D+jimukyoku)+was+supposed+to+smooth+these+bumps+in+the+road,+but+the+appointment+of+the+Ise+Shrines%E2%80%99+Chief+Priest+as+its+head+implied+that+Shinto+as+a+whole+should+center+on+Ise.+The+Head+Priest+of+the+Izumo+Shrine,+Senge+Takatomi+(1845%E2%80%931918),+challenged+that+idea+in+the+Pantheon+Dispute.&source=bl&ots=ePJp6y1JXS&sig=ACfU3U1vvWlDLshv_64QypQr5vdEAXk_yA&hl=en&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwiV-uf164yHAxVGn44IHXk1DcsQ6AF6BAgLEAM#v=onepage&q=While%20the%20shrine%20priests%20struggled%20to%20establish%20these%20churches%20in%20order%20to%20support%20their%20proselytizing%20as%20Instructors%2C%20however%2C%20the%20absence%20of%20public%20funding%20revealed%20a%20contradiction%20with%20the%20rhetoric%20of%20shrines%20as%20kokka%20no%20s%C5%8Dshi%2C%20public%20facilities%20providing%20the%20nation%E2%80%99s%20rites%20and%20creed.%20If%20the%20funding%20had%20to%20be%20arranged%20entirely%20on%20a%20private%20basis%2C%20then%20what%20foundation%20was%20there%20for%20claiming%20that%20shrines%20had%20a%20public%20status%3F%20The%20Shinto%20Office%20(Shint%C5%8D%20jimukyoku)%20was%20supposed%20to%20smooth%20these%20bumps%20in%20the%20road%2C%20but%20the%20appointment%20of%20the%20Ise%20Shrines%E2%80%99%20Chief%20Priest%20as%20its%20head%20implied%20that%20Shinto%20as%20a%20whole%20should%20center%20on%20Ise.%20The%20Head%20Priest%20of%20the%20Izumo%20Shrine%2C%20Senge%20Takatomi%20(1845%E2%80%931918)%2C%20challenged%20that%20idea%20in%20 [[ja:神社教会]] '''Shrine Churches (神社教会, jinja kyōkai)''' were a kind of Shinto organization that existed in the [[Meiji period]]. They emerged as a significant organizational model in Japan during the Meiji period, influenced by the interplay between Buddhist and Shinto clergy and the policies of the [[Great Promulgation Campaign]].<ref name=":0" />
== Early Collaboration and Withdrawal == In the early years of the Great Promulgation Campaign, Buddhist and Shinto clergy worked together to promote national religious unity. However, this cooperation ended in 1875 when Buddhist sects began to withdraw. Shimaji Mokurai, an influential Shinshū priest, played a key role in this shift. After traveling to Europe, Mokurai became convinced that the state should remain separate from religion and tolerate diverse religious beliefs. His persistent petitions led to the Shinshū sect’s withdrawal from the Campaign. Similarly, in 1876, the Kurozumikyō and Shintō Shūseiha movements also received permission to withdraw. These withdrawals caused resentment among Campaign leaders, who viewed these sects as undermining Shinto’s image of unity.<ref name=":0" />
== Shift to Shinto Dominance == With the departure of the Buddhists, the Campaign became distinctly "Shinto" in character. In response, the government established the Shinto Office (神道事務局, Shintō jimukyoku) in 1875, headed by the Chief Priest of the Ise Shrines, to replace the Great Teaching Institute. This marked the beginning of state management over Shinto shrines, contrasting with the autonomous operation of Buddhist and other religious organizations outside the Campaign.<ref name=":0" />
== Financial Challenges and Formation of Shrine Churches == By 1876, there were over 10,000 National Instructors, but the lack of public funding posed significant challenges. The central government’s decision not to provide financial support led to the development of a new type of Shinto organization: the Shrine Church (教会, kyōkai). These churches were attached to shrines and organized by territory-based parishes, independently funded by their members. Priest-Instructors led these churches, promoting Shinto funerals and ancestral rites to parishioners, thus reducing reliance on Buddhist temples. However, performing funerals was problematic for Shinto priests due to the pollution of death, which conflicted with Shinto purity laws. By serving as Instructors, priests could separate their funeral duties from their shrine roles, alleviating this issue.<ref name=":0" />
== Jingūkyōkai and Its Expansion == The Ise Grand Shrines played a crucial role in this new organizational model by establishing Jingūkyōkai in 1872. Organized by Urata Nagatami and Tanaka Yoritsune, Jingūkyōkai included ministers from former pilgrim masters who also became Instructors. By 1875, Jingūkyōkai had 304,704 members and operated a network of 1,600 preaching stations nationwide, often located in existing shrines. The organization presented Amaterasu as an agricultural deity and distributed talismans and almanacs from the Ise Grand Shrines. Jingūkyōkai ministers frequently addressed large audiences, numbering in the thousands.<ref name=":0" />
== Ideological and Financial Contradictions == Despite these efforts, the absence of public funding revealed contradictions in the rhetoric of shrines as public institutions providing national rites and creeds. The appointment of the Ise Shrines’ Chief Priest to head the Shinto Office implied that Shinto should center on Ise, a notion challenged by the Head Priest of the Izumo Shrine, Senge Takatomi, during the Pantheon Dispute.<ref name=":0">https://books.google.ca/books?id=ybljDQAAQBAJ&pg=PA380&lpg=PA380&dq=While+the+shrine+priests+struggled+to+establish+these+churches+in+order+to+support+their+proselytizing+as+Instructors,+however,+the+absence+of+public+funding+revealed+a+contradiction+with+the+rhetoric+of+shrines+as+kokka+no+s%C5%8Dshi,+public+facilities+providing+the+nation%E2%80%99s+rites+and+creed.+If+the+funding+had+to+be+arranged+entirely+on+a+private+basis,+then+what+foundation+was+there+for+claiming+that+shrines+had+a+public+status?+The+Shinto+Office+(Shint%C5%8D+jimukyoku)+was+supposed+to+smooth+these+bumps+in+the+road,+but+the+appointment+of+the+Ise+Shrines%E2%80%99+Chief+Priest+as+its+head+implied+that+Shinto+as+a+whole+should+center+on+Ise.+The+Head+Priest+of+the+Izumo+Shrine,+Senge+Takatomi+(1845%E2%80%931918),+challenged+that+idea+in+the+Pantheon+Dispute.&source=bl&ots=ePJp6y1JXS&sig=ACfU3U1vvWlDLshv_64QypQr5vdEAXk_yA&hl=en&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwiV-uf164yHAxVGn44IHXk1DcsQ6AF6BAgLEAM#v=onepage&q=While%20the%20shrine%20priests%20struggled%20to%20establish%20these%20churches%20in%20order%20to%20support%20their%20proselytizing%20as%20Instructors%2C%20however%2C%20the%20absence%20of%20public%20funding%20revealed%20a%20contradiction%20with%20the%20rhetoric%20of%20shrines%20as%20kokka%20no%20s%C5%8Dshi%2C%20public%20facilities%20providing%20the%20nation%E2%80%99s%20rites%20and%20creed.%20If%20the%20funding%20had%20to%20be%20arranged%20entirely%20on%20a%20private%20basis%2C%20then%20what%20foundation%20was%20there%20for%20claiming%20that%20shrines%20had%20a%20public%20status%3F%20The%20Shinto%20Office%20(Shint%C5%8D%20jimukyoku)%20was%20supposed%20to%20smooth%20these%20bumps%20in%20the%20road%2C%20but%20the%20appointment%20of%20the%20Ise%20Shrines%E2%80%99%20Chief%20Priest%20as%20its%20head%20implied%20that%20Shinto%20as%20a%20whole%20should%20center%20on%20Ise.%20The%20Head%20Priest%20of%20the%20Izumo%20Shrine%2C%20Senge%20Takatomi%20(1845%E2%80%931918)%2C%20challenged%20that%20idea%20in%20the%20Pantheon%20Dispute.&f=false</ref>

[[ja:神社教会]] '''Shrine Churches (神社教会, jinja kyōkai)''' were a kind of Shinto organization that existed in the [[Meiji period]]. They emerged as a significant organizational model in Japan during the Meiji period, influenced by the interplay between Buddhist and Shinto clergy and the policies of the [[Great Promulgation Campaign]].<ref name=":0" />
== Early Collaboration and Withdrawal == In the early years of the Great Promulgation Campaign, Buddhist and Shinto clergy worked together to promote national religious unity. However, this cooperation ended in 1875 when Buddhist sects began to withdraw. Shimaji Mokurai, an influential Shinshū priest, played a key role in this shift. After traveling to Europe, Mokurai became convinced that the state should remain separate from religion and tolerate diverse religious beliefs. His persistent petitions led to the Shinshū sect’s withdrawal from the Campaign. Similarly, in 1876, the Kurozumikyō and Shintō Shūseiha movements also received permission to withdraw. These withdrawals caused resentment among Campaign leaders, who viewed these sects as undermining Shinto’s image of unity.<ref name=":0" />
== Shift to Shinto Dominance == With the departure of the Buddhists, the Campaign became distinctly "Shinto" in character. In response, the government established the Shinto Office (神道事務局, Shintō jimukyoku) in 1875, headed by the Chief Priest of the Ise Shrines, to replace the Great Teaching Institute. This marked the beginning of state management over Shinto shrines, contrasting with the autonomous operation of Buddhist and other religious organizations outside the Campaign.<ref name=":0" />
== Financial Challenges and Formation of Shrine Churches == By 1876, there were over 10,000 National Instructors, but the lack of public funding posed significant challenges. The central government’s decision not to provide financial support led to the development of a new type of Shinto organization: the Shrine Church (教会, kyōkai). These churches were attached to shrines and organized by territory-based parishes, independently funded by their members. Priest-Instructors led these churches, promoting Shinto funerals and ancestral rites to parishioners, thus reducing reliance on Buddhist temples. However, performing funerals was problematic for Shinto priests due to the pollution of death, which conflicted with Shinto purity laws. By serving as Instructors, priests could separate their funeral duties from their shrine roles, alleviating this issue.<ref name=":0" />
== Jingūkyōkai and Its Expansion == The Ise Grand Shrines played a crucial role in this new organizational model by establishing Jingūkyōkai in 1872. Organized by Urata Nagatami and Tanaka Yoritsune, Jingūkyōkai included ministers from former pilgrim masters who also became Instructors. By 1875, Jingūkyōkai had 304,704 members and operated a network of 1,600 preaching stations nationwide, often located in existing shrines. The organization presented Amaterasu as an agricultural deity and distributed talismans and almanacs from the Ise Grand Shrines. Jingūkyōkai ministers frequently addressed large audiences, numbering in the thousands.<ref name=":0" />
== Ideological and Financial Contradictions == Despite these efforts, the absence of public funding revealed contradictions in the rhetoric of shrines as public institutions providing national rites and creeds. The appointment of the Ise Shrines’ Chief Priest to head the Shinto Office implied that Shinto should center on Ise, a notion challenged by the Head Priest of the Izumo Shrine, Senge Takatomi, during the Pantheon Dispute.<ref name=":0">https://books.google.ca/books?id=ybljDQAAQBAJ&pg=PA380&lpg=PA380&dq=While+the+shrine+priests+struggled+to+establish+these+churches+in+order+to+support+their+proselytizing+as+Instructors,+however,+the+absence+of+public+funding+revealed+a+contradiction+with+the+rhetoric+of+shrines+as+kokka+no+s%C5%8Dshi,+public+facilities+providing+the+nation%E2%80%99s+rites+and+creed.+If+the+funding+had+to+be+arranged+entirely+on+a+private+basis,+then+what+foundation+was+there+for+claiming+that+shrines+had+a+public+status?+The+Shinto+Office+(Shint%C5%8D+jimukyoku)+was+supposed+to+smooth+these+bumps+in+the+road,+but+the+appointment+of+the+Ise+Shrines%E2%80%99+Chief+Priest+as+its+head+implied+that+Shinto+as+a+whole+should+center+on+Ise.+The+Head+Priest+of+the+Izumo+Shrine,+Senge+Takatomi+(1845%E2%80%931918),+challenged+that+idea+in+the+Pantheon+Dispute.&source=bl&ots=ePJp6y1JXS&sig=ACfU3U1vvWlDLshv_64QypQr5vdEAXk_yA&hl=en&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwiV-uf164yHAxVGn44IHXk1DcsQ6AF6BAgLEAM#v=onepage&q=While%20the%20shrine%20priests%20struggled%20to%20establish%20these%20churches%20in%20order%20to%20support%20their%20proselytizing%20as%20Instructors%2C%20however%2C%20the%20absence%20of%20public%20funding%20revealed%20a%20contradiction%20with%20the%20rhetoric%20of%20shrines%20as%20kokka%20no%20s%C5%8Dshi%2C%20public%20facilities%20providing%20the%20nation%E2%80%99s%20rites%20and%20creed.%20If%20the%20funding%20had%20to%20be%20arranged%20entirely%20on%20a%20private%20basis%2C%20then%20what%20foundation%20was%20there%20for%20claiming%20that%20shrines%20had%20a%20public%20status%3F%20The%20Shinto%20Office%20(Shint%C5%8D%20jimukyoku)%20was%20supposed%20to%20smooth%20these%20bumps%20in%20the%20road%2C%20but%20the%20appointment%20of%20the%20Ise%20Shrines%E2%80%99%20Chief%20Priest%20as%20its%20head%20implied%20that%20Shinto%20as%20a%20whole%20should%20center%20on%20Ise.%20The%20Head%20Priest%20of%20the%20Izumo%20Shrine%2C%20Senge%20Takatomi%20(1845%E2%80%931918)%2C%20challenged%20that%20idea%20in%20the%20Pantheon%20Dispute.&f=false</ref>

[[ja:神社教会]] '''Shrine Churches (神社教会, jinja kyōkai)''' were a kind of Shinto organization that existed in the [[Meiji period]]. They emerged as a significant organizational model in Japan during the Meiji period, influenced by the interplay between Buddhist and Shinto clergy and the policies of the [[Great Promulgation Campaign]].<ref name=":0" />
== Early Collaboration and Withdrawal == In the early years of the Great Promulgation Campaign, Buddhist and Shinto clergy worked together to promote national religious unity. However, this cooperation ended in 1875 when Buddhist sects began to withdraw. Shimaji Mokurai, an influential Shinshū priest, played a key role in this shift. After traveling to Europe, Mokurai became convinced that the state should remain separate from religion and tolerate diverse religious beliefs. His persistent petitions led to the Shinshū sect’s withdrawal from the Campaign. Similarly, in 1876, the Kurozumikyō and Shintō Shūseiha movements also received permission to withdraw. These withdrawals caused resentment among Campaign leaders, who viewed these sects as undermining Shinto’s image of unity.<ref name=":0" />
== Shift to Shinto Dominance == With the departure of the Buddhists, the Campaign became distinctly "Shinto" in character. In response, the government established the Shinto Office (神道事務局, Shintō jimukyoku) in 1875, headed by the Chief Priest of the Ise Shrines, to replace the Great Teaching Institute. This marked the beginning of state management over Shinto shrines, contrasting with the autonomous operation of Buddhist and other religious organizations outside the Campaign.<ref name=":0" />
== Financial Challenges and Formation of Shrine Churches == By 1876, there were over 10,000 National Instructors, but the lack of public funding posed significant challenges. The central government’s decision not to provide financial support led to the development of a new type of Shinto organization: the Shrine Church (教会, kyōkai). These churches were attached to shrines and organized by territory-based parishes, independently funded by their members. Priest-Instructors led these churches, promoting Shinto funerals and ancestral rites to parishioners, thus reducing reliance on Buddhist temples. However, performing funerals was problematic for Shinto priests due to the pollution of death, which conflicted with Shinto purity laws. By serving as Instructors, priests could separate their funeral duties from their shrine roles, alleviating this issue.<ref name=":0" />
== Jingūkyōkai and Its Expansion == The Ise Grand Shrines played a crucial role in this new organizational model by establishing Jingūkyōkai in 1872. Organized by Urata Nagatami and Tanaka Yoritsune, Jingūkyōkai included ministers from former pilgrim masters who also became Instructors. By 1875, Jingūkyōkai had 304,704 members and operated a network of 1,600 preaching stations nationwide, often located in existing shrines. The organization presented Amaterasu as an agricultural deity and distributed talismans and almanacs from the Ise Grand Shrines. Jingūkyōkai ministers frequently addressed large audiences, numbering in the thousands.<ref name=":0" />
== Ideological and Financial Contradictions == Despite these efforts, the absence of public funding revealed contradictions in the rhetoric of shrines as public institutions providing national rites and creeds. The appointment of the Ise Shrines’ Chief Priest to head the Shinto Office implied that Shinto should center on Ise, a notion challenged by the Head Priest of the Izumo Shrine, Senge Takatomi, during the Pantheon Dispute.<ref name=":0">https://books.google.ca/books?id=ybljDQAAQBAJ&pg=PA380&lpg=PA380&dq=While+the+shrine+priests+struggled+to+establish+these+churches+in+order+to+support+their+proselytizing+as+Instructors,+however,+the+absence+of+public+funding+revealed+a+contradiction+with+the+rhetoric+of+shrines+as+kokka+no+s%C5%8Dshi,+public+facilities+providing+the+nation%E2%80%99s+rites+and+creed.+If+the+funding+had+to+be+arranged+entirely+on+a+private+basis,+then+what+foundation+was+there+for+claiming+that+shrines+had+a+public+status?+The+Shinto+Office+(Shint%C5%8D+jimukyoku)+was+supposed+to+smooth+these+bumps+in+the+road,+but+the+appointment+of+the+Ise+Shrines%E2%80%99+Chief+Priest+as+its+head+implied+that+Shinto+as+a+whole+should+center+on+Ise.+The+Head+Priest+of+the+Izumo+Shrine,+Senge+Takatomi+(1845%E2%80%931918),+challenged+that+idea+in+the+Pantheon+Dispute.&source=bl&ots=ePJp6y1JXS&sig=ACfU3U1vvWlDLshv_64QypQr5vdEAXk_yA&hl=en&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwiV-uf164yHAxVGn44IHXk1DcsQ6AF6BAgLEAM#v=onepage&q=While%20the%20shrine%20priests%20struggled%20to%20establish%20these%20churches%20in%20order%20to%20support%20their%20proselytizing%20as%20Instructors%2C%20however%2C%20the%20absence%20of%20public%20funding%20revealed%20a%20contradiction%20with%20the%20rhetoric%20of%20shrines%20as%20kokka%20no%20s%C5%8Dshi%2C%20public%20facilities%20providing%20the%20nation%E2%80%99s%20rites%20and%20creed.%20If%20the%20funding%20had%20to%20be%20arranged%20entirely%20on%20a%20private%20basis%2C%20then%20what%20foundation%20was%20there%20for%20claiming%20that%20shrines%20had%20a%20public%20status%3F%20The%20Shinto%20Office%20(Shint%C5%8D%20jimukyoku)%20was%20supposed%20to%20smooth%20these%20bumps%20in%20the%20road%2C%20but%20the%20appointment%20of%20the%20Ise%20Shrines%E2%80%99%20Chief%20Priest%20as%20its%20head%20implied%20that%20Shinto%20as%20a%20whole%20should%20center%20on%20Ise.%20The%20Head%20Priest%20of%20the%20Izumo%20Shrine%2C%20Senge%20Takatomi%20(1845%E2%80%931918)%2C%20challenged%20that%20idea%20in%20the%20Pantheon%20Dispute.&f=false</ref>
